evals.prompt: Does this skill explain how to do real refraction / lensing glass (not just blur), and how it degrades when the browser ignores the filter?
evals.expected_output: Points at the L3 fidelity level: a `.glass--l3` rule that adds `url(#glass-refract)` to the backdrop-filter, an inline SVG filter with feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap, and a loud caveat that backdrop-filter url() is Chromium-leaning while Safari/Firefox ignore the url() and gracefully degrade to L2 blur with no breakage.

assertions.text: Defines the L3 refraction level class
References the displacement filter id from the contract
Uses feDisplacementMap for the lensing effect
Uses feTurbulence to generate the noise map
Documents the Safari/Firefox graceful-degradation caveat

evals.prompt: How do I use this glass material with Tailwind v4 instead of writing the raw CSS by hand?
evals.expected_output: Shows the Tailwind v4 CSS-first approach: an `@theme` block defining `--blur-glass`, `--color-glass-tint`, `--radius-glass`, a custom `@utility glass` that pairs `-webkit-backdrop-filter` with `backdrop-filter`, and a vanilla-class to utility-class mapping table (backdrop-blur-md / bg-white/10 / border / rounded / shadow), plus the L3 url() arbitrary-value note.

assertions.text: Uses the Tailwind v4 @theme block
Defines a custom @utility named glass
Declares the --blur-glass theme token
Declares the --color-glass-tint theme token
Declares the --radius-glass theme token
Pairs -webkit-backdrop-filter with backdrop-filter

evals.prompt: I'm in a React + TypeScript app. Give me the React way to drop in a glass card and a glass button with the pointer-tracking specular highlight.
evals.expected_output: Points at assets/Glass.tsx and references/react.md: typed `<GlassCard>` and `<GlassButton>` wrappers that emit the contract classes (zero glass logic), a `useGlassPointer` hook that sets `--mx`/`--my` on pointermove guarded by prefers-reduced-motion, a `<GlassFilter id="glass-refract" />` mounting the SVG defs once, props level/prominent/theme/interactive/as/className, and a DRY note that the components wrap classes only.

assertions.text: Exports a typed GlassCard component
Exports a GlassButton component
Provides the useGlassPointer ref hook
Mounts the SVG defs via GlassFilter
Sets the pointer custom properties --mx/--my
Guards pointer tracking behind prefers-reduced-motion
Components emit contract classes only

evals.prompt: I'm putting glass panels on a LIGHT background and the edges of the panels disappear. What's the gotcha and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the #1 light-glass gotcha: a translucent white rim is invisible on a light backdrop, so the `[data-glass-theme="light"]` preset must DARKEN the border (use a dark rgb rim instead of white), denser white tint, softer shadow, less saturate. Also ties into contrast-on-glass / WCAG guidance for text legibility.

assertions.text: Provides a light theme preset via the data attribute
Overrides the rim/border token in the light preset
Calls out the low-contrast border gotcha explicitly

evals.prompt: How does the skill keep glass readable in browsers that don't support backdrop-filter? Show me the fallback strategy.
evals.expected_output: Describes the fallback-first pattern: the base `.glass` rule paints a legible solid-ish translucent fill (rgb tint at ~.6 alpha) with no backdrop-filter, then an `@supports ((backdrop-filter: blur(1px)) or (-webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(1px)))` block upgrades to the translucent fill + paired backdrop-filter / -webkit-backdrop-filter. So no-support browsers get a readable panel, not unreadable text on full transparency. Also mentions prefers-reduced-transparency / prefers-contrast solid fallbacks.

assertions.text: Uses @supports to feature-detect backdrop-filter
Feature-detects both prefixed and unprefixed backdrop-filter
Honors prefers-reduced-transparency for a solid fallback
Honors prefers-contrast for a solid fallback
Base rule is legible without backdrop-filter support

evals.prompt: In plain CSS, build a frosted-glass stat card for a dark dashboard — translucent, with the blurred background showing through. Give me the CSS.
evals.expected_output: A layered glass card (NOT just blur): a `.glass` base with fallback-first solid fill, `backdrop-filter: blur() saturate()` ALWAYS paired with `-webkit-backdrop-filter`, a translucent tint fill, a hairline rim border, an outer box-shadow for elevation, `position: relative; isolation: isolate;` so pseudo-element sheen anchors, and a `.glass-card` geometry layer. Should reach for L2 specular (inset highlight + inner depth + ::before sheen) and reference the token custom properties rather than hardcoding. Wrapped in an @supports upgrade for graceful fallback.

assertions.text: Defines the base .glass material class
Provides the .glass-card geometry layer
Uses backdrop-filter with blur and saturate
Always pairs -webkit-backdrop-filter
Anchors pseudo sheen with position/isolation on base
Drives the look from custom-property tokens
Layers a specular highlight beyond plain blur

evals.prompt: My `.card { backdrop-filter: blur(10px) }` shows nothing / looks wrong. Why, and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Diagnoses the common backdrop-filter failures and fixes: (1) missing `-webkit-backdrop-filter` for Safari/WebKit, (2) the element needs a translucent (not opaque, not zero-alpha) background for the blur to read, (3) no contrasting content actually sitting BEHIND the element, (4) a non-isolated stacking context, (5) the element/ancestor opacity or filter trap. Should push toward the full layered material + the @supports fallback-first pattern, not a single blur declaration.

assertions.text: Flags the missing -webkit-backdrop-filter pairing
Explains the translucent-background requirement
Mentions content must sit behind the glass to show through
Provides the @supports fallback-first remedy

evals.prompt: Build a React glass card component with an Apple-style liquid look (the iOS 26 Liquid Glass vibe) on the web.
evals.expected_output: Uses the React layer from this skill: a `<GlassCard>` (and optionally `<GlassPanel>`/`<GlassButton>`) from assets/Glass.tsx with `level` (1|2|3) and `interactive` props to reach the L2/L3 specular + pointer-tracking liquid look, mounts `<GlassFilter id="glass-refract" />` once for L3 refraction, relies on glass.css as the single source of truth (components emit classes only), and respects prefers-reduced-motion. Should NOT reinvent the glass CSS inline in the component, and should note the iOS↔web pairing with liquid-glass-design.

assertions.text: Provides a GlassCard React wrapper
Supports the level prop for fidelity 1|2|3
Supports an interactive prop for pointer specular
Mounts the SVG refraction filter via GlassFilter
Components emit contract classes, no inline glass CSS
Cross-links the iOS liquid-glass-design counterpart

evals.prompt: How do I make two glass blobs merge into one another like the iOS Liquid Glass mercury effect on the web?
evals.expected_output: Covers Tier A and Tier B morphing: Tier A matched-geometry morph (framer-motion layoutId shown by example as the glassEffectID analog, or a vanilla FLIP helper) for a glass element flowing between states; Tier B gooey merge via an SVG `#glass-goo` filter (feGaussianBlur -> feColorMatrix alpha-crank) on a `.glass-goo` container so overlapping shapes snap into one silhouette, with loud caveats about composing the gooey filter with backdrop-filter and perf. Notes Tier C (continuous liquid refraction through the merge) needs WebGL and is out of scope.

assertions.text: Provides the gooey-merge container class
Uses feGaussianBlur in the gooey filter
Uses feColorMatrix to crank the merged alpha
Documents the matched-geometry morph (layoutId or FLIP)
Flags Tier C continuous refraction as WebGL/out-of-scope
